sound_type: high thin 'tseet' contact call -- nearly ultrasonic, barely audible to humans, 8,000-20,000+ Hz, high thin contact note between individuals in nocturnal canopy, E=3, old-growth habitat
ecosystem: old-growth coniferous forest. Nests in tree cavities in large-diameter trees. Old-growth dependent -- requires mature forest structure with cavity-bearing trees. Strictly nocturnal.
season: year-round. Does NOT hibernate. Active all year but strictly nocturnal. Less active in severe winter weather but does not enter torpor.

species_pair.pair_type: Sciuridae OCTET -- EIGHTH consecutive Sciuridae (75-76-77-78-79-80-81-82). The OCTET. LONGEST family run in engine history: EIGHT. Three marmots (Marmota, 75-77) + five squirrels (Urocitellus 78, Callospermophilus 79, Tamiasciurus 80, Sciurus 81, Glaucomys 82). Six genera. Eight habitats. Alpine to old-growth.

coordinates.description: Reference for old-growth coniferous forest habitat in the Cascade Range, Washington. Mature Douglas-fir and western hemlock forest with cavity-bearing trees.

taxonomy_note.description: EIGHTH CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE (75-82). Sciuridae octet. LONGEST family run: EIGHT. NEW GENUS Glaucomys -- sixth Sciuridae genus. Flying squirrels. Twenty-first mammalian entry. Nineteenth mammalian species. Ninth Rodentia. Sciuridae's eighth entry. First GLIDING mammal.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Glaucomys from Greek glaukos ('silver/gray') + mys ('mouse'). sabrinus from Latin Sabrinus, relating to the Severn River. Described by Shaw (1801).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Glaucomys -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Sixth Sciuridae genus after Marmota (75-77), Urocitellus (78), Callospermophilus (79), Tamiasciurus (80), Sciurus (81). Flying squirrels -- the first gliding genus.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- ninth Rodentia in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- twenty-first mammalian entry. Nineteenth mammalian species.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's EIGHTH entry at degree 82. Eighth consecutive (75-82). The OCTET. LONGEST family run: EIGHT.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 8,000-20,000+ Hz. Nearly ultrasonic. At the upper edge of human hearing.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Approximately 10,000-18,000 Hz. Many humans cannot hear this frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. HIGH THIN 'TSEET' CONTACT CALL. Soft, brief, nearly ultrasonic contact notes between individuals.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 0.05-0.15 seconds per note. Very brief. Barely detectable.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single thin notes. High-pitched 'tseet.' Brief and soft. The quietest vocalization in the Sciuridae octet.
song_metrics.note_types: Tseet (high thin contact call), soft chitter (social), squeak (distress).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Simple: isolated single notes or short series of contact calls. No sustained vocalization.
song_metrics.timbre: Thin, high, ethereal. Nearly ultrasonic. Like the highest note on a piccolo but softer. Barely audible to most humans.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Very soft. Audible only at close range and only to listeners with good high-frequency hearing. Many people cannot hear it at all.
song_metrics.tempo: Slow. Individual contact notes with significant pauses. Not rapid or sustained.
song_metrics.context: CONTACT CALLS between individuals in nocturnal canopy. Social coordination in darkness. Not territorial aggression.
song_metrics.key_property: HIGH THIN 'TSEET' CONTACT CALL -- the 83rd acoustic element. Nearly ultrasonic. 8,000-20,000+ Hz. E=3. Barely audible. Nocturnal old-growth. The quietest and highest vocalization in the Sciuridae octet.

physical_description.overall: Small nocturnal gliding squirrel. 110-230 g (4-8 oz). 25-37 cm total length including tail. Soft dense fur. Large dark eyes for nocturnal vision. Patagium (gliding membrane) from wrist to ankle. Flattened tail for steering.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 82: (1) PATAGIUM -- skin membrane from wrist to ankle visible when gliding or when arms extended. The defining feature. (2) LARGE DARK EYES -- enormous eyes for nocturnal vision. (3) SOFT DENSE FUR -- silky, cinnamon-brown above, white below. (4) FLATTENED TAIL -- used as rudder during glides. (5) STRICTLY NOCTURNAL -- rarely seen during day.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Soft, dense, silky. Cinnamon-brown to olive-brown above. Distinctly paler than Douglas Squirrel. BELLY: White to cream -- clean white underside. PATAGIUM: Furred. Visible as loose skin along flanks. TAIL: Flattened dorsoventrally. Used for steering in glides.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Adapted for soft foods (truffles, lichens, fungi).
physical_description.field_id: Almost never seen due to nocturnal habits. If seen at night: a small squirrel with enormous dark eyes gliding silently between trees, or perched on a branch with loose patagium visible along flanks. White belly. Silent or nearly so. If you hear a barely audible high 'tseet' in old-growth forest at night, it may be a Northern Flying Squirrel.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Octet (75-82) -- EIGHT Consecutive!
nodes.description: Eight consecutive Sciuridae. The OCTET. Longest family run in engine history: EIGHT. Three marmots + five squirrels. Six genera. Eight habitats. Alpine to old-growth.

nodes.title: High Thin 'Tseet' Contact Call -- 83rd Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Nearly ultrasonic contact call. 8,000-20,000+ Hz. E=3. Barely audible to humans. Brief, soft, ethereal. Nocturnal canopy communication. Quietest in the Sciuridae octet.

nodes.title: First Gliding Mammal -- The Patagium
nodes.description: Patagium (skin membrane wrist-to-ankle) enables glides of 20-50 meters between trees. First 'flying' mammal in the engine. The engine goes airborne. Steering via flattened tail.

nodes.title: Old-Growth Dependent -- Cavity Nester
nodes.description: Requires old-growth coniferous forest with cavity-bearing trees. Nests in tree cavities. Shares old-growth dependence with Pacific Wren (1) and Pileated Woodpecker (54). Old-growth logging is primary threat.

nodes.title: Truffle Mutualism -- American Marten Connection (65)
nodes.description: Both eat truffles (hypogeous fungi). Both disperse mycorrhizal spores through fecal pellets. Truffle mutualism connects species across families and degrees. Essential for old-growth forest health.

nodes.title: Northern Spotted Owl Prey -- Key Ecological Link (SPS-004)
nodes.description: The Northern Spotted Owl PREYS ON FLYING SQUIRRELS. Both old-growth dependent. Both nocturnal. The owl needs the squirrel. The squirrel needs the old-growth. Predator-prey in the nocturnal canopy.

nodes.title: Pileated Woodpecker Cavity Connection (SPS-054)
nodes.description: Pileated Woodpecker creates cavities in large trees. Northern Flying Squirrel nests in cavities. The woodpecker's work becomes the squirrel's home. Cavity ecosystem linkage.

nodes.title: The Death Cab Acoustic Parallel
nodes.description: Both E=3. Both quieter versions of something bigger. Both nocturnal/intimate. Both reveal hidden dimensions. Acoustic intimacy meets nocturnal old-growth.

nodes.title: Twenty-Second Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-two consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. CSV reliability continues.

cross_references.relationship: SCIURIDAE OCTET. Urban park (81) to old-growth (82). Introduced (81) to native (82). Diurnal (81) to nocturnal (82). Bark+chatter (E=4) to tseet (E=3). Maximum contrast within consecutive Sciuridae.
TRUFFLE MUTUALISM! Both eat truffles. Both disperse mycorrhizal spores. Marten (65) also preys on Douglas Squirrel (80). Cross-family truffle network. Essential for old-growth forest health.
NORTHERN SPOTTED OWL PREYS ON FLYING SQUIRRELS -- key ecological link! Both old-growth dependent. Both nocturnal. Predator-prey in the nocturnal canopy. The owl needs the squirrel.
OLD-GROWTH SHARED. Both depend on old-growth forest structure. Pacific Wren in the understory, Flying Squirrel in the canopy. Two old-growth specialists across 82 degrees.
CAVITY CONNECTION. Pileated Woodpecker creates cavities in large trees. Northern Flying Squirrel nests in cavities. The woodpecker's work becomes the squirrel's home.
